questions.text: A critical bug is found an hour before release. What do you do?

answers.text: Jump in and fix it yourself — you're fastest
Walk the engineer through debugging it so they learn
Take command of the situation — triage, delegate, and clear the path
Revert the risky change and ship what's stable now

questions.text: How do you know a project is on track?

answers.text: I talk to people — their tone and energy tell me everything
Dashboards, burndown charts, and status reports
Are we shipping increments regularly? Then we're fine
I check that every open question has an owner and a deadline

questions.text: A junior engineer is stuck on a task. How do you help?

answers.text: Pair with them and show them how to do it
Ask questions until they find the answer themselves
Point them to the right documentation and check back later
Reassign it — put the right person on the right problem

questions.text: Someone proposes replacing a core system with new technology. Your reaction?

answers.text: We tried that in 2019. Let me tell you what happened.
Let's prototype it in a week and see if it actually works
Show me the migration plan, rollback strategy, and success metrics
If the team's excited about it, that energy is worth exploring

questions.text: How do you prefer to work on a big problem?

answers.text: Give me the problem, close the door, and I'll come back with an answer
Break it into small pieces and start shipping the first one today
Understand the full scope before writing a single line of code
Figure out who's confused about what and start clarifying

questions.text: What frustrates you most at work?

answers.text: Nobody knows who's responsible for what
People keep reinventing wheels that already work fine
We over-plan and under-ship
We ship sloppy work and call it 'iterating'

questions.text: Your team has a conflict between two senior engineers. What do you do?

answers.text: Talk to each privately, read the real dynamic, then bring them together
Make the call yourself — someone has to decide
Set up a structured decision framework so it's not personal
Stay steady and keep both sides focused on the shared goal

questions.text: A feature is 90% done but the last 10% is painful. What now?

answers.text: Ship the 90% — users get value now, we polish later
Finish it properly — the last 10% is what separates good from great
I'll just knock out that last 10% myself this weekend
Identify exactly what's blocking and assign clear owners for each piece

questions.text: What's your meeting style?

answers.text: Agenda, time-boxed, decisions documented, next actions assigned
I avoid meetings — just send me the doc and I'll comment
Casual and conversational — the best info comes out when people relax
Short and decisive — here's the problem, here's what we're doing, go

questions.text: Your company announces a major reorg. Your first thought?

answers.text: Who's going to be confused? Let me get ahead of the chaos
I've seen this movie before — I know how it ends
Let me talk to my people and find out how they're really feeling
New structure, new opportunities — where's the most interesting problem now?

questions.text: You need to communicate a difficult decision to the team. How do you deliver it?

answers.text: I craft a carefully worded message — every sentence is deliberate
I tell it straight — here's what we're doing and why, no sugarcoating
I frame it as the next chapter in our story — where we've been and where we're going
I tailor the message differently for each audience — engineers, PMs, execs

questions.text: You join a new company. What do you do in your first two weeks?

answers.text: Wander around — sit in on meetings, read old docs, talk to everyone I can find
Question everything — why is this process so slow? Why do we use this tool?
Find the messiest problem and start proposing fixes immediately
Map out the org chart, learn who has influence, and build relationships

questions.text: What kind of project excites you most?

answers.text: A brand-new product built from scratch with a small team
Taking a slow, bloated system and making it 10x faster
A complex cross-team initiative where I'm the one connecting the dots
One where I get to write the vision doc that rallies everyone behind it

questions.text: A meeting is going in circles. What do you do?

answers.text: Calmly restate the core question and redirect the group
Summarize the two positions in one sentence each and force a decision
Just make the decision myself — we've talked enough
Suggest we take it offline and let people think before we reconvene

questions.text: How do you present a postmortem?

answers.text: As a narrative — what happened, how it felt, what it taught us
A clean timeline with root causes distilled to a single paragraph
I focus on making sure the right people hear the right takeaways
I turn it into process changes — new alerts, new runbooks, new owners

questions.text: A legacy process is slowing everyone down. Your move?

answers.text: Rip it out and replace it — fresh start
Understand why it exists first — someone created it for a reason
Measure the bottleneck, then surgically optimize the worst parts
Write up a clear proposal comparing the old way and new way

questions.text: Your skip-level wants a summary of your team's quarter. How do you prepare?

answers.text: I tell the story of the quarter — the arc from challenge to outcome
One page, three bullet points, done
I know exactly what this exec cares about and I frame everything around that
I write a beautifully clear document that speaks for itself

questions.text: What's your instinct when you see a team you're not on struggling?

answers.text: Walk over and ask what's going on — maybe I can help or learn something
Stay in my lane — they have their own leadership
Quietly connect them with the right people who can help
Offer to rebuild their tooling — it's probably a systems problem

questions.text: How do you handle a high-pressure room where tempers are flaring?

answers.text: Be the calm center — lower my voice, slow the pace, ground the room
Cut through the emotion — state the facts and force us back to the issue
Read the room — figure out what's really driving the tension
Take charge — assign actions and end the meeting

questions.text: If you could change one thing about your organization tomorrow, what would it be?

answers.text: Kill the sacred cows — remove outdated practices nobody questions
Build a new internal tool that automates the biggest time-waster
Rewrite the company narrative so everyone understands where we're headed
Explore adjacent teams and create bridges between silos
